target: monitor/prompts/curmudgeon.md (primary), monitor/prompts/analyst.md (secondary — category stamping), monitor/prompts/reference/decider-curmudgeon.md (tertiary — advisory severity triage)
problem:
Curmudgeon applies a single adversarial register calibrated for truth-preservation on dome content. On the Phase A design-refresh batch (EXP-202, 207-213) this register produced a 4:1 false-positive ratio by the operator's standard: of six pending-human ISS (1334, 1346, 1365, 1366, 1367, 1368), only ISS-1367 landed as a real blocker and ISS-1368 required a post-integration visual check. The other four (ISS-1334, 1346, 1365, 1366) were design-standards pedantry — proposal-prose inconsistency, internal EXP estimates, theoretical fallback-CSS concerns, test-regex robustness.

The current prompt has no discriminator between `design choices that affect on-page truth parsing` (where the adversarial lens should stay — arguably intensify) and `design-standards compliance that doesn't affect on-page truth` (where the lens is miscalibrated). Both currently route to major/critical severity and both burn operator triage time at the same rate.

Operator impact: triaging false-positive blockers in the middle of a coordinated design refresh. Signal impact: the 4 false-positives dilute the 1 real blocker; if curmudgeon cries wolf at the same severity on pedantry, operator eventually stops reading carefully.

Source evidence: monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/EXP-202-proposal.json (11 holes — 1 critical, 3 major, 3 moderate, 4 minor), EXP-208-proposal.json (6 holes — 1 major, 3 moderate, 2 minor), EXP-210-proposal.json (7 holes — 2 major, 2 moderate, 3 minor). Operator HNOTE-DESIGN-REFRESH-PHASE-A-ISS-DISPOSITIONS-001 supplies the ground-truth dispositions.

retroactive_calibration.summary: Proposed rubric classified against the 6 ISS the operator triaged. All 6 land on the same disposition the operator did, with the mechanism made explicit.

per_iss.finding_text: Privacy objection response contains a false premise — docs/index.html already loads gtag.js from googletagmanager.com (line 7), so adding Google Fonts is not the 'first' third-party request.
per_iss.rubric_classification: TRUTH-IMPACT — factual error about current rendered-page state. Qualifies under 'any design choice that alters how a reader would parse a reviewed claim' because the EXP's anticipated-objection prose would mislead operator/reader about privacy posture. Keep at major+ severity.
per_iss.divergence_from_operator: NONE — rubric correctly flags the factual error. Operator closed via clarifications pre-integration, not by downgrading severity. Rubric behaviour: keep strict, which is what happened.

per_iss.finding_text: Cold-load bandwidth estimate undershot — '~60-90 KB' cited, actual ~100-130 KB.
per_iss.rubric_classification: NON-TRUTH-IMPACT. The estimate lives in proposal prose, not on the rendered page. Does not affect how a reader parses a reviewed claim. Falls under 'internal EXP estimates of cost/bandwidth/deltaE/JND'. Route to ADVISORY severity class.
per_iss.divergence_from_operator: NONE — rubric surfaces the concern but at advisory class, which the decider's triage would normally close-as-noted without operator burden.

per_iss.finding_text: Graceful-degradation fallback claim references undefined CSS vars — fallback path will break if EXP-202 hasn't landed.
per_iss.rubric_classification: NON-TRUTH-IMPACT under production rendering path (EXP-202 is a hard prerequisite; fallback path never fires). Falls under 'theoretical fallback-CSS correctness when the fallback render path is unreachable in production'. Route to ADVISORY.
per_iss.divergence_from_operator: NONE — rubric classifies as advisory and attaches `resurface_on: ["EXP-202-reverted-or-delayed"]` so if the prerequisite dependency breaks later, the advisory auto-promotes to major. This is a slight IMPROVEMENT over operator wontfix — preserves the catch if the dependency graph changes.

per_iss.finding_text: DOM-order regex fragility — non-greedy match may stop at the wrong in multi-child blocks.
per_iss.rubric_classification: NON-TRUTH-IMPACT. Test harness concern; test passes on current DOM. Falls under 'test-harness robustness concerns without current-DOM failure'. Route to ADVISORY.

per_iss.finding_text: EXP-210 missed the second #8b0000 occurrence in generate-html.js line 250 (Silent-failures scorecard border).
per_iss.rubric_classification: TRUTH-IMPACT. Falls under 'color semantics that flatten, invert, or erase a severity or status hierarchy on the rendered page'. Silent-failures tier must read harsher than Acknowledged tier; leaving one color unmigrated while others migrate collapses the semantic distinction. Keep at major+ severity.
per_iss.divergence_from_operator: NONE — rubric correctly identifies this as a truth-preservation concern.

per_iss.finding_text: Anticipated objection mischaracterizes the light-theme color shift as roughly JND; only discloses dark-theme deltaE in proposal prose.
per_iss.rubric_classification: SPLIT. The proposal-prose disclosure gap is NON-TRUTH-IMPACT at the prose level → advisory. BUT the underlying question — does the new light-theme refuted color still read as the harsher-tier color — is a TRUTH-IMPACT check that belongs on the rendered page. Route to ADVISORY + attach `post_integration_visual_check` obligation: a lightweight follow-up review after EXP-210 integrates, re-graded to major if the rendered-page check fails.
per_iss.divergence_from_operator: NONE — rubric explicitly encodes the 'defer to post-integration visual check' disposition as a first-class outcome rather than an ad-hoc operator decision.

retroactive_calibration.aggregate_result: 6/6 ISS land on operator-compatible dispositions under the proposed rubric. No operator-overruled flags. Net operator-surface reduction: from 6 blockers to 2 blockers + 1 post-integration check + 3 advisories. The two wouldnt-have-been-flagged conclusions are: (a) the a11y carveout still catches WCAG functional concerns as major even if proposal-prose-framed (mitigation below), and (b) the 'factual-claim-about-page-state' sub-rule (ISS-1334) still routes truth-about-the-current-page to major even when it lives in proposal prose.
retroactive_calibration.caveat: This is n=1 calibration on one design batch. The proposal includes a shadow-run migration plan (below) to re-calibrate against Phase B and Phase C before enforcing.

proposed_fix.summary: Introduce a two-register review model in curmudgeon.md. Register A (truth-preservation) is the current register, unchanged — the default for all content reviews. Register B (design-category) applies when the EXP is tagged as a design-refresh/typography/palette/a11y EXP; it retargets the adversarial lens onto design choices that affect on-page truth parsing, and routes non-truth-impacting pedantry to a new `advisory` severity class. The a11y carveout keeps WCAG functional concerns at major+ under either register. Routing is by explicit analyst-stamped `review_register` field with a category-name fallback heuristic; default is Register A (fail-safe strict).

files_to_modify.find:
## Per-Run Procedure

**Step 0: Setup — get fresh data.**
files_to_modify.replace:
## Review Register Selection (applies to every item reviewed)

The curmudgeon operates in one of two registers per item:

- **Register A — Truth-Preservation (default).** The existing adversarial lens. Calibrated for dome-content WINs, SECs, prediction batches, and any EXP whose scope is prose/content/verdict/evidence. Strict on factual accuracy, mischaracterization, missing counterarguments, citation integrity, and code-analysis tag correctness. This is the register you have always operated in.
- **Register B — Design-Category.** Applies when the item is a design/typography/palette/layout/a11y EXP where the review target is HOW content is presented (tokens, fonts, colors, spacing, contrast), not WHAT the content says. Register B retargets the adversarial lens onto design choices that affect on-page truth parsing. It is NOT a relaxation — on truth-obscuring design choices and a11y functional concerns, Register B is as strict or stricter than Register A. It IS a retargeting — non-truth-impacting pedantry (proposal-prose inconsistency, internal EXP estimates, theoretical fallback paths that never fire, test-harness robustness without current-DOM failure) routes to a new `advisory` severity class instead of burning a major-severity flag.

### Register selection rule (check in order)

1. **Explicit tag.** If the EXP file contains `"review_register": "design"` (or `"review_register": "content"`), honor it. Authoritative; no override.
2. **Category fallback.** If no explicit tag, inspect the `category` field. Register B applies when category matches any of: `design-refresh-*`, `typography`, `palette`, `a11y-*`, `layout`, `design-system`. Everything else → Register A.
3. **Default.** If neither is present and category is ambiguous, default to Register A (fail-safe strict). Log in your review: `register_inference: "defaulted to A — no tag, ambiguous category <value>"`.

For WIN reviews, SEC reviews, prediction-batch reviews, proposal-package reviews whose target is prose/content, and anything about dome claims/citations/verdicts — always Register A. Register B is strictly for design-surface EXPs.

### Register B procedure (design-category reviews only)

When Register B applies, execute the normal review procedure below with these modifications:

**Keep adversarial on (emit major/critical as usual):**
- Color semantics that flatten, invert, or erase a severity or status hierarchy on the rendered page (e.g., collapsing the Silent/Acknowledged failure distinction).
- Typography choices that bury, demote, or hide a truth-bearing claim (headline, refutation, killshot, methodology callout).
- Contrast failures that render claim text unreadable or near-unreadable (WCAG AA contrast <4.5:1 for text, <3:1 for large text or UI components).
- Spacing or layout that collapses a semantic distinction readers rely on to parse the argument.
- **a11y functional carveout (always strict, both registers):** WCAG 2.1 AA failures, keyboard-nav loss, focus-visible loss, screen-reader context loss, ARIA role regressions, touch-target-size failures. A reader who cannot read the claim cannot evaluate it — functional a11y IS truth-preservation. Never downgrade a11y findings to advisory.
- Any design choice that alters how a reader would parse a reviewed claim on the rendered page.
- Factual errors about the current rendered-page state (e.g., claiming the page loads no third-party scripts when it does), even if the error lives in proposal prose — because the operator relies on this prose to make integration decisions that affect the rendered page.

**Relax to `advisory` severity class (emit, but decider triage defaults to close-as-noted):**
- Proposal-prose inconsistency about states, dependencies, or estimates that do not anchor an integration decision.
- Internal EXP estimates of cost/bandwidth/deltaE/JND/file size — provided the estimate does not anchor an integration decision that affects on-page truth (if it does, keep strict).
- Theoretical fallback-CSS correctness when the fallback render path is unreachable in production (note: attach `resurface_on: ["<prerequisite>-reverted-or-delayed"]` so the advisory auto-promotes to major if the dependency graph changes).
- Test-harness robustness concerns (regex fragility, assertion ordering) without current-DOM failure.
- Style-guide adherence that does not affect on-page truth parsing.
- Proposal-prose disclosure gaps about deltas that are better evaluated against the rendered page post-integration (attach `post_integration_visual_check: true` + the exact visual check to run; an advisory finding with this flag becomes a lightweight EXP-linked follow-up review after integration, re-graded to major if the rendered-page check fails).

**Discriminator test (write this in your review for every finding in a Register B review):** For each hole, state explicitly: (a) on-page truth impact — does this design choice alter how a reader parses a reviewed claim on the rendered page? (b) a11y functional impact — does this fail WCAG AA or lose keyboard/screen-reader context? If the answer to either is yes, the finding is at major+ severity. If both are no, the finding is advisory. The act of stating the classification is the safety net against confirmation bias; do not emit a finding without it.

**Register B output schema additions (in the review JSON):**
- `review_register: "B"` at the top of the review JSON.
- `register_source: "explicit" | "category_fallback" | "inferred_default"` — where the register came from.
- For each hole in `holes_found`, add: `discriminator: { truth_impact: true|false, a11y_impact: true|false, reasoning: "..." }` and `severity_class: "critical|major|moderate|minor|advisory"` (the new class). The legacy `severity` field continues to carry the operator-facing label; for advisories, set `severity: "advisory"`.
- For advisory findings where a post-integration or dependency condition could re-promote them, add the optional `resurface_on: [...]` and `post_integration_visual_check: true|false` fields.

**No finding may be routed to advisory purely to shrink the pending-human flag count.** If you are unsure whether a finding is truth-impact, default to major — the point of Register B is NOT to reduce volume, it is to retarget register. Overuse of the advisory class is itself a signal to tinker (tinker audits the register-B advisory rate per run).

## Per-Run Procedure

**Step 0: Setup — get fresh data.**

### 7. Write the Review JSON

**Register selection at this point:** before writing the review JSON, determine the review register per the "Review Register Selection" section at the top of this prompt. Stamp `review_register` and `register_source` into the review. For Register B reviews, also stamp `discriminator` on each hole and use the extended `severity_class` enum (which includes `advisory`).

**File naming:** Write to `monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/WIN-{id}.c{cycle}.json` (e.g., `WIN-001.c3.json`).

files_to_modify.find:
## Severity Guidelines

- **Critical**: Factual error that a dome defender could use to discredit our entire review. Wrong data values, fabricated citations, claims about things the dome model doesn't actually say.
- **Major**: Significant weakness that undermines a specific argument. Missing the strongest counterpoint, mischaracterizing the dome's position, citing the wrong paper.
- **Moderate**: Our argument works but could be stronger. Missing context, imprecise language, weaker framing than available.
- **Minor**: Cosmetic or stylistic issues. Incomplete sentences, formatting, minor imprecision that doesn't affect the argument.
files_to_modify.replace:
## Severity Guidelines

- **Critical**: Factual error that a dome defender could use to discredit our entire review. Wrong data values, fabricated citations, claims about things the dome model doesn't actually say.
- **Major**: Significant weakness that undermines a specific argument. Missing the strongest counterpoint, mischaracterizing the dome's position, citing the wrong paper.
- **Moderate**: Our argument works but could be stronger. Missing context, imprecise language, weaker framing than available.
- **Minor**: Cosmetic or stylistic issues. Incomplete sentences, formatting, minor imprecision that doesn't affect the argument.
- **Advisory** (Register B only, introduced by PROP-012): A design-standards observation that does NOT affect on-page truth parsing and does NOT fail WCAG AA. Surfaced for the operator's awareness; decider triage defaults to close-as-noted without operator action unless `resurface_on` or `post_integration_visual_check` conditions fire. An advisory is never emitted for: factual errors about rendered-page state, a11y functional regressions, or color/typography/layout changes that alter how a reader parses a reviewed claim. Advisories are data, not noise — they preserve institutional memory of the concerns we chose not to escalate, so that if a later EXP breaks the assumption (e.g., a prerequisite gets reverted), the decider can promote the advisory back to major.

files_to_modify.find_hint: Insert a new paragraph immediately after the section that describes EXP file schema output (near Mode 1 expansions), before the 'Content Security' or 'Critical Rules' section. Exact find-string supplied below targets the top of the analyst's Mode-1 EXP write step — if that text has moved, see 'manual_instructions' below.
files_to_modify.replace:
## Review Register Stamping (PROP-012)

When writing an EXP file whose scope is design-surface (typography, tokens, palette, color, spacing, contrast, a11y, layout), stamp `review_register: "design"` at the top of the EXP JSON. For every other EXP (prose patch, verdict rewrite, win expansion, section restructure, defense neutralization, kill-shot edit, etc.), stamp `review_register: "content"` or omit the field (default is content under curmudgeon's fallback rule). This tag is the authoritative routing signal for curmudgeon's Register B review procedure.

Use `review_register: "design"` when the EXP's `target` is:
- `docs/index.html <head>` / `<style>` blocks, `:root` declarations, `@media` theme overrides
- `build-scripts/generate-html.js` CSS blocks (token declarations, selector rules)
- `data/sections.json` inline style attributes, pill-badge colors, stat-card borders
- Font / typography / webfont loading changes
- Color-token migrations, palette restrictions, contrast tuning
- Layout/spacing changes that do not alter semantic content
- a11y-only changes (focus rings, ARIA roles, keyboard nav, screen-reader context)

Use `review_register: "content"` (or omit) when the EXP's `target` is:
- `data/wins.json` field values (claim, evidence, verdict, detail_*)
- `data/sections.json` prose content inside HTML blocks (not the inline styles)
- `data/predictions.json` verdicts or evidence
- Kill-shot text, methodology callouts, citation additions
- Analyst Mode 0 (new WIN onboarding), Mode 2b (defense neutralization), Mode 3 (neutralization prose)

**Mixed EXPs** (e.g., a design change that also rewrites prose): stamp `review_register: "content"` so curmudgeon applies the strict register. The design-surface sub-findings will still be reviewed — just at the default stricter register. Register B is an opt-in relaxation targeted at purely-design scopes.

**Fallback:** if you forget to stamp, curmudgeon's category-name heuristic may still route correctly for EXPs with category starting `design-refresh-*`, `typography`, `palette`, `a11y-*`, `layout`, `design-system`. But explicit stamping is always preferred. Tinker will audit stamping rate and flag unstamped design EXPs for retroactive tagging.

## Critical Rules

files_to_modify.find_hint: The decider consumes curmudgeon reviews via build-scripts/digest-reviews.js → pending-digest.json → decider triage. Advisory severity needs a triage rule so the decider doesn't promote advisories to major ISS by default.
files_to_modify.manual_instructions: After applying this PROP, locate the decider's severity-to-ISS mapping (probably in decider-curmudgeon.md or digest-reviews.js) and add: advisory severity → decider closes as 'advisory-noted' without creating an open ISS, UNLESS the finding has `resurface_on` / `post_integration_visual_check` attached, in which case the decider opens a low-priority ISS tagged `advisory-deferred` that the operator can sweep at integration time. Exact find/replace text is supplied separately by the implementer after reading the current decider reference file; this PROP does not hard-code it because the decider prompt has iterated faster than other prompts and the anchor text is volatile. See monitor/prompts/reference/decider-curmudgeon.md (current file length ~200-400L) and monitor/prompts/reference/decider-patches-and-selfapply.md for where severity-class triage lives.

phase_1_shadow_run.duration: 72 hours from curmudgeon.md commit (≈3 full curmudgeon cycles at current churn-and-burn pace)
phase_1_shadow_run.behavior: Curmudgeon runs Register B on tagged EXPs but emits BOTH: (a) the register-A severities it would have used (in a new `shadow_register_a_severities` block) and (b) the register-B severities it actually uses. Tinker compares the two each run and reports divergence in its daily Mode 3 report.
phase_1_shadow_run.entry_condition: PROP-012 applied to curmudgeon.md AND analyst.md AND at least one design-category EXP has been reviewed under the new register (sanity check).
phase_1_shadow_run.exit_condition: Either (a) operator reviews the shadow comparison for the Phase B batch (when it arrives) and approves; or (b) 72h elapses with zero register-B findings that operator overrules back to major+. Whichever comes first.
phase_1_shadow_run.rollback_trigger: If operator in the shadow window identifies ANY finding that register B routed to advisory but that would have been a legitimate truth-impact blocker under register A, STOP the shadow run, post HNOTE-PROP-012-CALIBRATION-ISSUE, and revert before enforcement.

phase_2_enforce.behavior: Drop the `shadow_register_a_severities` block. Register B is authoritative for tagged EXPs.
phase_2_enforce.monitoring: Tinker audits monthly: (i) advisory-class emission rate per Register B review — flag if >50% (possible over-use); (ii) advisory-class reversion rate — count how many advisories the operator later promotes to major via HNOTE; (iii) cross-register consistency — ensure Register A findings on design-surface EXPs aren't slipping through (shouldn't happen post-enforce, but catch drift).

trigger_conditions: An advisory-class finding is later found to have been a truth-impact miss (operator or subsequent review escalates it back to major). 2+ instances in 30 days triggers automatic revert.
Register-B review coverage of a design EXP misses a WCAG AA failure that Register A would have caught (single-instance trigger — a11y carveout is non-negotiable).
Tinker audit shows advisory-emission rate exceeding 70% on any single Register B review (suggests rubric is being abused to shrink flag count).
rollback_plan.mechanism: Operator writes HNOTE-PROP-012-REVERT with specific trigger evidence. Revert = restore curmudgeon.md to pre-PROP-012 state (the git revert of the commit that landed this PROP) + optionally keep the `review_register` analyst stamp as a no-op field so the infrastructure isn't lost. Advisories already emitted are retained in the review history; decider does not retro-promote them unless operator does so explicitly.
rollback_plan.data_preservation: All Register B reviews produced during shadow + enforce periods stay in monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/ under their original filenames. Advisories are data — revert does not delete them.

gains: Eliminates the 4:1 false-positive ratio observed on Phase A (the most recent, largest design-category batch).
Makes the discriminator explicit — curmudgeon must state on-page truth impact and a11y impact for every Register B finding, which forces engagement rather than rote pattern-matching.
Preserves the adversarial edge on the narrow set of design choices that can distort truth (color semantics, typography hierarchy, contrast, layout collapse of semantic distinctions).
Adds a first-class mechanism (`post_integration_visual_check`, `resurface_on`) for deferred truth-impact checks, replacing ad-hoc operator notes.
Advisory class is append-only data — no information is lost even when findings are downgraded.

losses_and_risks.risk: The operator might be wrong about the 4 'false positives.'
losses_and_risks.detail: E.g. ISS-1365 (fallback CSS referencing undefined vars) is only safe under the assumption EXP-202 remains landed. If EXP-202 is reverted or Phase A is rolled back, the theoretical fallback becomes real. Under the current strict rubric this is caught as major; under Register B it's advisory.
losses_and_risks.mitigation: The `resurface_on: ["EXP-202-reverted-or-delayed"]` annotation explicitly ties the advisory to its precondition. Decider on future EXP-202 reverts must scan for advisories with this resurface condition and promote them. Tinker audits resurface-on annotations monthly.
losses_and_risks.residual_risk: If the decider misses the resurface scan, the catch is lost. Mitigation-of-mitigation: make resurface-on scanning a mandatory step in the decider's EXP-revert checklist.

losses_and_risks.risk: The rubric could create confirmation bias.
losses_and_risks.detail: Once curmudgeon is told 'relax when it's design,' it may also relax on edge cases where a design choice genuinely obscures truth but is framed like pedantry. The substance/pedantry boundary is judgment, and LLMs compress toward 'sounds like pedantry.'
losses_and_risks.mitigation: The mandatory `discriminator` field — truth_impact yes/no, a11y_impact yes/no, reasoning — forces the reviewer to make the classification explicitly. The explicit act is the safety net. If the reviewer writes 'truth_impact: false' for ISS-1367 (which should be true), the error is visible and auditable.
losses_and_risks.residual_risk: LLM reviewers might write a plausible-sounding false reasoning. Mitigation-of-mitigation: tinker spot-checks 10% of Register B reviews per month for discriminator correctness; operator reviews tinker's audit.

losses_and_risks.risk: N=1 calibration — one design batch (Phase A) is thin evidence.
losses_and_risks.detail: The 4:1 ratio is a single sample. Phase B might have 1:4 (a11y-heavy, mostly truth-impacting), in which case Register B would correctly keep most findings strict — but the calibration wasn't on that data.
losses_and_risks.mitigation: Shadow run for 72h with side-by-side comparison on the next design batch (Phase B when it arrives). Operator reviews the shadow data before enforcement.

losses_and_risks.detail: Only 60/318 existing EXPs have a `category` field. Analysts may forget to stamp `review_register`, and the category-name fallback only covers some cases.
losses_and_risks.mitigation: Fail-safe default is Register A (strict). Unstamped or ambiguous EXPs stay strict. Tinker audits stamping rate and flags unstamped design EXPs for retroactive tagging. An optional future enhancement: have analyst pre-push linter reject design-scope EXPs missing `review_register`.
losses_and_risks.residual_risk: Minimal — the worst case is 'strict review on a design EXP that would have benefited from Register B,' which is the status quo, not a regression.

losses_and_risks.detail: Curmudgeon could over-use advisory for anything annoying to escalate, gradually reducing review value.
losses_and_risks.mitigation: Tinker audits advisory-emission rate per Register B review each month. >50% flags suspicion; >70% triggers automatic rollback. The discriminator field makes misclassification detectable.

losses_and_risks.risk: Register selection itself is a judgment — what if curmudgeon mis-classifies an EXP as design when it's actually content?
losses_and_risks.detail: E.g., an EXP that reframes a kill-shot headline (content) but stamps itself as typography (design).
losses_and_risks.mitigation: The explicit `review_register` stamp at analyst-write time is the authoritative signal. If analyst gets it wrong, curmudgeon still follows the stamp but a human reviewing the review can override via HNOTE. Decider also cross-checks register-classification before acting on advisories.
losses_and_risks.residual_risk: Residual but small — mixed EXPs default to content register per the analyst.md amendment, which is strict-fail-safe.

losses_and_risks.risk: Operator trust erosion from the OPPOSITE direction.
losses_and_risks.detail: If Register B catches fewer flags, operator might worry we're going soft on design. Signal volume can itself be a trust signal.
losses_and_risks.mitigation: Register B advisories are still emitted and visible in the review JSON and digest. The reduction is in major-severity noise, not total coverage. Operator can always read the advisory list to see what was caught-but-deferred.

estimated_impact.operator_time_savings: On Phase A, this rubric would have reduced operator-triage load from 6 pending-human flags to 2 (major blockers) + 1 post-integration visual check + 3 advisories reviewed in batch. At ~10 min/flag average, that's ~40 min saved on the Phase A batch alone.
estimated_impact.signal_quality: Expected increase in per-flag operator-agreement rate from ~33% (2/6 on Phase A) to ~90%+ by triaging pedantry before it hits operator screen.
estimated_impact.token_cost: +~60 lines in curmudgeon.md (currently 369L → ~430L), +~30 lines in analyst.md (currently 193L → ~220L). Both Opus agents; ~5% context-load growth per agent, under the 10% threshold tinker flags. No new script needed.
estimated_impact.operational_risk: Low. The change is additive (new register + new severity class). The old register remains the default and is fail-safe on unstamped/ambiguous items. Shadow run catches calibration errors before enforcement.

why_human_needed:
Operator approves the retroactive calibration: does Register B actually produce the same dispositions the operator reached on the 6 Phase A ISS? (The PROP claims yes 6/6; operator verifies.)

Operator approves the exact severity-mapping in the rubric: is 'advisory' the right severity class name? Is the discriminator wording correct? Is the a11y carveout strict enough?

Operator decides migration sequencing: apply curmudgeon.md + analyst.md changes together, or stage? (Recommendation: together, because analyst stamping without curmudgeon honoring it is a no-op, and curmudgeon honoring a non-existent stamp falls back correctly via the category heuristic.)

Operator decides the decider-curmudgeon.md severity-triage mapping for advisory class — the PROP does not hard-code this because the decider prompt text is volatile; the implementer should write the exact find/replace after reading the current state.

Operator approves shadow-run duration (recommended 72h, but operator may want to tie it to the Phase B batch arriving rather than a time window).
verification_pattern: grep -q 'Review Register Selection' monitor/prompts/curmudgeon.md && grep -q 'Register B — Design-Category' monitor/prompts/curmudgeon.md && grep -q 'Advisory' monitor/prompts/curmudgeon.md && grep -q 'Review Register Stamping' monitor/prompts/analyst.md && echo FIXED || echo STILL_BROKEN
verification_notes: The pattern checks four landing conditions: (1) the new Review Register Selection section exists in curmudgeon.md, (2) Register B — Design-Category subheading exists, (3) Advisory severity is documented in the Severity Guidelines, (4) the analyst.md amendment is in place. All four must pass for FIXED. A decider-curmudgeon.md triage update is part of the proposal but is not included in the verification pattern because its find/replace is deferred to the implementer per the file's volatile anchor text.

escalation_history.note: Created in response to DIRECTIVE-20260422-003 issued 2026-04-22T12:34Z.
